narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60—which is below the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 34, lower than the usual 36.70. The admissions model predicted a 58% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. This prediction hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers—far below the average of around 5.5—and strongly signaled to the model a higher risk of bar exam struggles. Your LSAT score, while not your strongest area, actually worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing, as it showed some competitive potential compared to lower performers. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA pulled slightly in the positive direction, suggesting foundational strengths despite being under the norm. Overall, your lower Year 3 performance outweighed these positives, painting a picture riskier than that of typical admitted students, who often rank in the middle or top of their classes by that point. The model saw your situation as more challenging than most, where stronger late-stage academics align closely with bar success. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal drive, especially given barriers many Black students like yourself face in competitive academic environments. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score further, strengthening your Year 3 record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these key areas could shift the model's view dramatically, opening doors at schools that value your unique background. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by targeting these improvements—your next steps can rewrite this story.
